title: Putting Children First: How Low‐Wage Working Mothers Manage Child Care, by Ajay Chaudry
abstract: Putting Children First: How Low-Wage Working Mothers Manage Child Care. Ajay Chaudry. New York: Russell Sage Foundation. 2004. 341 pp. ISBN 0-87154-171-8. $39.95 (hardback). In Putting Children First, which is based on 3 years of fieldwork with 42 low-income mother-child pairs in New York City, Ajay Chaudry shows how low-income working mothers are actually forced to put children second. The topic is urgent because after the welfare reform of 1996, low-income mothers of young children are expected to work, and their work rates have risen rapidly. Chaudry selected study participants like the mothers affected by welfare reform. Most of the mothers he followed had received cash welfare assistance at some time, and during his fieldwork, most received assistance such as food stamps, public housing, and child-care subsidies. The mothers were all less-skilled workers, earned $9.27/hour on average, and held an average of 3.4 different jobs in the 4 years studied. Most worked full time. Chaudry's investigation of child-care types, mothers’ preferences, and causes of instability in care is an important contribution to understanding the influence of low-income mothers’ employment in young children's lives and the conditions of low-wage working women's mothering. Chaudry's research focused on care arrangements and reasons for changes, which he documented for each child from birth to age 4. The findings are scary. The children were in nonmaternal care for an average of 10 hours a day. Yet, they did not get the high-quality, stable care that children need for intellectual development and emotional security. Instead, mothers scrambled to keep their infants, toddlers, and preschoolers in complex, fluctuating patchworks of low-cost care arrangements in order to accommodate mothers’ long and frequently changing work schedules. In his sample, Chaudry identifies 215 separate “spells” of primary (nonmaternal) child care, an average of 5.12 spells per child in the first 4 years of life. More than one third (38%) of the care spells lasted between 0 and 3 months; fewer than one in five spells lasted longer than 12 months. Most mothers also used secondary care, such as hiring someone to bring a child from day care to home. The volatility in child care, Chaudry finds, was largely because of factors that pushed children out of particular arrangements. For example, 32% of exits occurred because care was too expensive or a mother feared for her child's well-being; alterations in family, housing, health, or work situations accounted for another 32% of exits. Less than one third of exits (28%) were voluntary moves to a mother's preferred option, such as a higher quality or age-appropriate arrangement. Findings on types of care include family members are low-cost providers, but as a primary arrangement, this type of care was the least reliable; licensed family child-care providers offered the most durable situations, in part, because they more often could cover long hours and nonstandard schedules; mothers often wanted, but did not get, educational care. Putting Children First aims to convey mothers’ points of view. Chaudry presents mothers’ stories in some detail and extensively quotes narrative evidence from in-depth interviews. The mothers’ statements about arranging child care compatible with their jobs, however, often seem lacking in narrative coherence. This fact, as much as the details the women convey, reflects the nonsense of expecting low-income mothers to be able to buy quality child care at market rates. Further, the women get little support. In New York City, Chaudry found, mechanisms for establishing eligibility for child-care subsidies were opaque and time consuming, vouchers to pay for child care given to women transitioning from welfare to work often arrived too late, and there were long waiting lists for licensed family child-care providers and subsidized center care such as Head Start. In a concluding chapter, Chaudry discusses policy options. His recommendations are rooted in the everyday lives of the women he studied but are also limited by this perspective. For example, he argues that mail, email, and telephone applications for subsidies are easier for working mothers to use and that policy should prioritize continuity of care. Yet, Chaudry does not consider at-home infant care (also known as AHIC) as an option, despite finding that low-income mothers would prefer to spend more time with their infants, and nonmaternal care options for young babies are in short supply. Unfortunately, Putting Children First suffers from blurry writing. Chaudry's tables and frequent lists are useful, while the chapter summaries and conclusions are unhelpfully vague and imprecise. Further, the mothers’ words, so copiously quoted, are disappointingly decontextualized. We learn nothing of the textures of the families’ lives. Chaudry's unusually detailed methods appendices will be very useful for anyone doing this kind of field research, but I recommend seeking other texts as models for presenting findings. Nonetheless, the research reported in Putting Children First should be of immediate relevance to scholars and policymakers interested in welfare reform, families, work, inequality, urban poverty, mothers, and children.
